+     * Add a lenient (CPS shadow) resolution entry. The {@code ResolveViewShadow} analyzer rule
+     * looks up entries in {@link AnalyzerContext#optionalLinkedResolution()} by the shadow's full
+     * {@code IndexPattern} (view name + applicable exclusions), so the same view referenced
+     * with different exclusion lists can be wired to different results.
+    public TestAnalyzer addLenientShadow(IndexPattern indexPattern, IndexResolution resolution) {
+     * Convenience overload of {@link #addLenientShadow(IndexPattern, IndexResolution)} for the
+     * common no-exclusion case: keys the entry by an {@link IndexPattern} built from
+     * {@code esIndex.name()} (which the test should match the local view name).
* Adds an index with empty resolution (used for pruned subqueries).
+// Regression: view_with_subquery's body is `FROM (FROM employees)` — a UnionAll wrapping a Subquery
+// of a UnresolvedRelation. When composed with an unrelated index in the outer query, the
+// view-compaction pass lifts the inner subquery's UnresolvedRelation up to the outer level and
+// would have merged it with the sibling UnresolvedRelation into a single combined pattern. If that
+// merge runs AFTER pre-analysis (i.e. as an analyzer rule before ResolveTable), the merged pattern
+// string is one PreAnalyzer never sent to field-caps and ResolveTable would silently produce an
+// unresolved relation. This test exercises the exact code path; it would fail with a verification
+// error (Unknown index) under that bad placement. See PR #147611 / #543 / #472.

+     * Each {@code ViewShadowRelation} represents a "if a remote project has an index with this
+     * view's name, treat it as if the user wrote a remote index reference at this position"
+     * lookup. The lenient field-caps integration (deferred to a follow-up PR) populates
+     * (view name + applicable exclusions). The full pattern is the lookup key — different
+     * exclusion lists at the same view name produce distinct {@code ViewShadowRelation}
+     * instances and may resolve differently (e.g. one comes back empty because of the
+     * exclusions, the other resolves to a remote index). This rule:
